title: Cancer statistics, 2024
source: CA: A Cancer Journal for Clinicians (CA Cancer J Clin), American Cancer Society
data_source: SEER program, National Cancer Institute (NCI); National Program of Cancer Registries (NPCR), CDC; North American Association of Central Cancer Registries (NAACCR); National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS)
key_conclusions: In 2024, 2,001,140 new cancer cases and 611,720 cancer deaths are projected to occur in the United States
Cancer mortality continued to decline through 2021, averting over 4 million deaths since 1991 due to reductions in smoking, earlier detection, and improved treatment

Incidence rates increased for 6 of the top 10 cancers: breast (0.6% annually), pancreas (1% annually), uterine corpus (1% annually), prostate (3% annually), liver in females (2% annually), kidney (1.5% annually), and HPV-associated oral cancers

Colorectal cancer incidence increased by 1-2% annually in adults younger than 55 years since the mid-1990s, making it the first leading cause of cancer death in men under 50 and second in women under 50
The 5-year relative survival rate for all cancers combined increased from 49% in the mid-1970s to 69% during 2013-2019

Wide persistent cancer disparities exist: compared to White people, mortality rates are two-fold higher for prostate, stomach and uterine corpus cancers in Black people and for liver, stomach, and kidney cancers in Native American people
Lung cancer death rates declined by 59% from the 1990 peak in men and 36% from the 2002 peak in women due to reduced smoking and treatment advances
The cancer death rate dropped 33% from 1991 through 2021, translating to an estimated 4.1 million fewer cancer deaths
